kept.text:
Most tokens launch on hype.
Very few launch on structure.

This is why @CerbAgent stands out.

Now live on Base  but the chain isn’t the story.
The design is.

No presale.
No team allocation.
Bonding curve price discovery.

That framework does something important it aligns incentives from block one.

No insider advantage.
No artificial price anchoring.
Just pure market-driven entry.

That kind of start doesn’t just look fair  it behaves differently.

And then there’s the product.

Cerberus isn’t building another dashboard or reactive alert tool.
It’s designing a multi-layered security system that operates across the full risk cycle:

→ Shield  proactive wallet scanning across chains
→ Sentinel  real-time transaction monitoring
→ Recovery  post-incident fund retrieval attempts

Before. During. After.

Three phases most tools treat separately  if they address them at all.

That integration is the edge.

Because in today’s environment, security is still largely reactive arriving only after exposure.

What @CerbAgent is attempting with $CERB is a shift toward continuity where protection isn’t a moment, but a system.

If execution matches the architecture, this doesn’t just improve security…

It redefines how it’s experienced.

kept.text:
𝐀𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐩𝐨𝐢𝐧𝐭, 𝐢𝐭’𝐬 𝐧𝐨𝐭 𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐭 𝐝𝐢𝐬𝐜𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐚𝐧𝐲𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞

It’s about what you’ve done with it so far.

After spending time with @QwertiAI, everything becomes clearer.

➤ You understand the product
➤ You’ve used the features
➤ You’ve built some level of activity

Example:

Someone just hearing about it today is still trying to figure things out.

But someone who has been active already knows where to click, what to do, and how to move.

That difference shows over time.

Because in early platforms, timing matters.

But what you do with that timing matters more.

Sometimes the real question is not “is it early?”

It’s “what have you done since you found it?”

kept.text:
The creator economy doesn't have a discovery problem. It has a structure problem.

Projects spend budgets on agencies. Agencies route money to established accounts. Those accounts post forgettable content. No verification, no accountability, no way to know if any of it worked. And somewhere in that chain, the people actually capable of explaining a product clearly the curious, informed, smaller-account creators got filtered out before they ever had a chance.
@RallyOnChain is the first system I've seen that fixes the structure instead of patching around it.
There are no follower thresholds. 

No agency middlemen. No opaque selection process. You write a clear, accurate, compelling post about a project, you submit it, and Rally's AI evaluates it across content quality, accuracy, originality, and real engagement metrics. 

The scoring weights are visible. The rewards are calculated on-chain and distributed transparently at the end of each campaign period.

A creator with 500 followers who genuinely understands what they're writing about can outperform an account with 50,000 followers posting hollow content. 

That isn't a marketing slogan. It's built directly into how the system scores and rewards.

That shift changes who shows up. And who shows up changes what gets built.

The community forming around Rally isn't here because of easy money. They're here because this model is correct. Influence has value. 

That value should go to the people generating it, not to intermediaries. Tracking that on-chain, evaluating it with AI, and making the whole thing verifiable isn't a feature list it's a new standard for how this industry should work.

Being early to a new standard is different from being early to a new product. Products get copied. Standards get adopted.

kept.text:
We’ve always been taught that there’s a hard line between life and death, but a groundbreaking discovery has just revealed a "Third State" that exists right in the middle. 

Researchers have found that even after an organism is technically "dead," its cells don't just stop. Instead, they can actually reorganize themselves into entirely new, multicellular forms that can move, react to their environment, and even repair themselves.

This isn't just a biological curiosity; it’s a total paradigm shift. Some scientists are now suggesting that this "Third State" hints at a level of cellular consciousness we never suspected. These cells aren't just surviving; they are actively cooperating to form new structures (like "Biobots") that can perform tasks independently of the original "host." It’s as if your cells have their own survival code that only kicks in once the main system shuts dow

kept.text:
GKite builders 🪁 🚀

AI Can Now Pay for APIs, Execute Tasks and Reason, I Proved It Today via Kite AI Passport 👇

Using @GoKiteAI Passport, I ran real paid agent flows, not demos.

What I tested :

Paid API discovery (Exa)
Real 402 payment triggers
Scoped spending sessions (tight budgets)
Passkey approval flow
Multi-step reasoning
Content generation

Full loop :
discover - pay - think - output

What worked perfectly :
Session creation + approval flow
Budget enforcement (hard limits respected)
Agent execution pipeline
Multi-step reasoning chain
Real-time paid API integration

👉 The system actually executes, not just responds. But I also hit something important :

During execution, I encountered : " session_asset_forbidden " 👀

Even though : session was valid, budget was correct and also approval succeeded

The transaction failed due to asset mismatch between catalog and execution layer.

Why this matters :
This isn’t a “UI bug.”
It’s a core coordination issue between : service catalog asset format, session policy asset definition and  runtime payment requirement

In agent economies, this layer is critical.

If agents can’t reliably resolve what asset to use, autonomy breaks.

What this system already gets right :

We’re no longer talking about : API keys, manual approvals and static workflows

We now have :
scoped agent identity
programmable money limits
automated execution paths
human-in-the-loop security (passkey)

That part is actually working and The real shift I saw :

AI is no longer just generating outputs.

It’s : discovering services, requesting payments, executing actions andreturning results

inside a controlled economic boundary.

We’re moving from : AI that talks to AI that transacts and acts

And systems like Kite AI Passport are one of the first real implementations where that loop is visible in production behavior even if some edge cases still surface.

Still testing & Still breaking things

I have also submitted via feedback website, what i faced, Thank you @0xLaughing , @Henryleemr 🪁 🫡

profile_bio.description:
💎Signal Hunter | Early Alpha Curator AI × Web3 | Ambassador @GoKiteAI🪁| Exploring to @Somnia_Network 🌌
 
Exploring • Building • Growth • NFA🙏

quoted_tweet.text:
I didn’t really expect Kite Passport to feel this real until I actually used it end-to-end

Started with a messy WSL + Codex setup, went through session creation, approvals and suddenly I wasn’t just “testing an AI tool” anymore…

I was actually letting an agent execute tasks with real constraints.

What I did :
 created spending sessions (budget + per-tx limits)
 approved them via passkey flow
 ran Codex as the execution layer
 used real services like Exa, Firecrawl and fal. ai inside those sessions

And the interesting part wasn’t the tools…

It was the control model

Identity - session - permission & execution

The agent doesn’t “guess” or “ask repeatedly”

It operates strictly inside boundaries you define :
budget, time & scope

Once I saw Firecrawl and fal. ai actually execute through that flow, it clicked :

this isn’t just AI tooling anymore…

it’s controlled agent execution infrastructure.

Still early, still evolving but the direction is obvious

We’re moving from AI that responds…to AI that acts under permissioned autonomy

and @GoKiteAI Passport is one of the clearest early versions of that shift I’ve tested 🤯

still diving in and exploring,btw really enjoying the experience so far and huge thanks to @0xLaughing  for the early access and the opportunity to explore it.

Higher 🪁 🔥

profile_bio.description:
💎Signal Hunter | Early Alpha Curator AI × Web3 | Ambassador @GoKiteAI🪁| Exploring to @Somnia_Network 🌌

kept.text:
Clarifying fact — the discovery in Volodymyr-Volynskyi (Volyn region).

This is a mass grave of victims of Soviet terror from 1940–1941, after the occupation under the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact. In 2011, remains of 367 people were exhumed there, including many civilians and children.

Destroying Russian propaganda, which tries to shift the blame onto Ukrainians:

The 14th Waffen-SS “Galicia” Division was formed only in 1943 (even ignoring the fact that they committed no crimes), while the mass grave in Volodymyr-Volynskyi dates to 1940–1941 — two years before the division was even created. The victims were mostly local Ukrainians and Poles, members of the intelligentsia. The Waffen-SS “Galicia” fought exclusively against the Red Army on the front lines, did not execute civilians, and was cleared by all tribunals and court proceedings.

I also checked whether the photo might be from Srebrenica, as some claim. It is not — all sources confirm it is from Ukraine. Besides, it doesn’t matter: the fact itself is real.

kept.text:
$GRAB

In the past 2 years, Grab has made several investments/acquisitions clustered around 3 main themes: autonomous mobility, financial services and delivery expansion.

1. Chope: Expanding F&B beyond delivery into discovery, bookings, dining deals and merchant tools.

 2. Nham24: Expansion into Cambodia, acquiring the dominant food/grocery delivery player in the country.

3. Everrise Supermarkets: Reinforcing Grab's grocery strategy and expanding footprint into East Malaysia.

4. WeRide: Chinese Robotaxi player

5. May Mobility: American Robotaxi player

6. Vay Technology: German Robotaxi player

7. Momenta: Chinese Robotaxi player

8. Infermove: AI-enabled robotics for first and last-mile delivery.

9. Stash Financial: US digital investing platform, to supplement GrabFin.

10. FoodPanda Taiwan: Expansion into Grab's 9th market, first outside SEA.

They have become much more active with external capital allocation, alongside $1B in cumulative buybacks announced. It still feels to me like there is one massive acquisition waiting to happen with the ~$5B on the balance sheet.

kept.text: ChatGPT’den akademik kaynak istemek bazen riskli olabiliyor; çünkü model kulağa gerçek gelen ama aslında var olmayan makale veya citation üretebiliyor. Literatür takibi için daha doğru yöntem, doğrudan akademik veri tabanlarına bağlı araçlar kullanmak. R Discovery bu noktada faydalı bir seçenek: ilgi alanına göre güncel makaleleri öneriyor, PubMed/CrossRef/OpenAlex gibi kaynaklardan literatür tarıyor, özet çıkarıyor ve okuma listesi oluşturmanı sağlıyor. Yani ChatGPT’yi fikir üretmek ve metinleri anlamak için kullan; ama gerçek kaynak bulma ve alanı takip etme işinde R Discovery gibi akademik discovery araçlarını merkeze koy.

tweet.text:
Just a recap of recent information discovery + likely mapping with $SIVE:
 
-> $JBL 1.6T
-> Lightmatter
-> Ayar
-> $MRVL Celestial
-> Lightelligence 
-> $POET 
-> $GFS ecosystem
-> $AMD CPO
-> O-Net / Enablence
-> $AAPL Silicon Photonics
_
-> $YSS Golden Dome/DoD
-> $RTX / $ERIC (Space)
-> Bae Systems
-> $AEVA 

With $JBL to Ayar feeding into hyperscalers like $MSFT, $GOOGL, $AMZN, $META.

With likely Lightelligence to O-Net feeding into Asian Hyperscalers like Tencent, Bytedance, and Baidu.

On top of that... the overarching TAM with CPO from the GS report goes from 0 ->  $91B. 

And Sivers happens to be the bleeding edge for CPO (also starting from 0). 

This is definitely high-beta and volatile. 

But if Win volume ramps alongside $SIVE, I see them both becoming $10B+ companies next year. 

This is just extremely early on (H1) before the CPO supercycle starts H2 2026.
